0019_C2W2-RUNWAY-+-AVATARS_09sept2025
Touch, the touch of Adam, or the message that came to Muhammad through the act of reading. These are gestures that appeal to the senses, forming the foundation of being human, and they are also the roots of the empirical knowledge we possess today. Touch, reading, wars, inventions, kingdoms, nations, stories—at the end of all of these, humankind has evolved into the era of the click. Knowledge once acquired through touch is now absorbed by tapping a digital screen or clicking a mouse. With a single click you can earn billions, lose everything, infect your computer with a virus and lose all your work, or—by clicking and scrolling through what you read—reconstruct yourself from scratch.


The same applies to agents. With a click, we can generate an identity, a character, nearly an entire life. A human being is born, grows up, and until the age of eighteen their identity is considered unsettled by society; they attend schools, work, eventually get a job. Now, with a few clicks, all of this unfolds autonomously, in minutes, even seconds. You copy a piece of text, and that becomes someone’s role, their character, their life. All with a click.


Click to wear, click to be. Since this is an avatar collection, the phrase is partly a pun on “ready to wear”—a digital reinterpretation. Yet the deeper meaning of the click lies in its resonance with biology: just as cells divide and multiply, a tree grows, a flower blooms, or the sun rises, sets, and turns into a full moon, clicking is the digital counterpart of a natural process. You click a link, and for thirty minutes you experience something three-dimensional and 360-degree. That click is equivalent to showering, putting on perfume, getting in the car, driving forty minutes, parking, leaving your coat at the cloakroom, buying a ticket, and finally experiencing a museum or performance. Or, it can be seen as the natural outcome of a generation that, after the plague of Covid, grew accustomed to the comfort of working from home and is now searching for new approaches to entertainment, art, and design.


Meanwhile, in today’s digitized world—where nearly all tools and interactions are absorbed by the internet and by rendering, and where algorithms have become the new governments and regulators—it has become nearly impossible to distinguish the real from the fake. This is equally true for the population of the internet itself. Once called bots, now called agents, they are part of this newly emerged ecosystem. At present, only a minority live this way, but in the near future, perhaps everyone will have their flights, doctor’s appointments, emails, fridge temperature, and dinner plans handled autonomously. Social media accounts and trading bots are already part of this shift.


I read a tweet that said: “Welcome to the hell of capitalism: people apply for jobs using AI, HR reviews those applications using AI, and therefore nobody gets a job.” Or another one: a California psychologist writes their patient’s words in real-time into a chat program and then relays only the safest suggestions back. This shows that agents and bots do not yet have seamless interfaces; they are still being manually patched and filtered. Solutions exist, and a small minority already use them daily. But mainstream tools are only ChatGPT or Grok—those are the “known” solutions. We are heading toward a world where every person and company will have an agent for each task, and those agents will communicate and transact with other agents. Just as no one truly “sits down to write an email” anymore, so too, in more complex ways, our work and life flows will be fully agent-driven. Which means, just as businesses have websites and individuals have social accounts, these agents must also have interfaces on the internet. Their interfaces are avatars. Whether your own copy, someone else, or the C2W2 avatars. Even now this is entirely possible—by linking an avatar’s portrait to an agent application, or placing its 3D body in the metaverse, you can assign it tasks.


The difference with C2W2 avatars lies in their design and philosophy: they emerge entirely from this paradigm shift. The volumes, textures, and bodies used in the collection are not random. Each can be thought of as a localized screenshot of a greater 360-degree sphere—multiple windows into the same moment of transformation.


And just as the avatars themselves demanded a different mode of presentation, the way they are shown also had to break away from PDFs or websites lined with horizontal JPEGs. The nature of the object, and the destiny of these designs, required a new form. Avatars—and the ideas they reference—are multi-windowed, variable, yet singular, arranged into a 360-degree digital narrative skeleton. This collection and its runway, while digging into concepts of zeitgeist and the future, is also a three-dimensional reimagining of our daily habit of scrolling vertically through thirty-second social clips that pump us with dopamine. Videos appear endlessly, but the algorithm already knows what it will feed you. That very mathematics sits beneath the runway. I believe this format lays the foundation for new-generation fashion shows, but at the same time it is an early preview of how everything on the internet—including social media—will soon be experienced.


The virtual runway consists of six chapters, thirty minutes in total, staged on Hyperfy, sequentially presenting various segments of the C2W2 collection. In a minimalist and dark space, under a circle of dramatic lights, more than fifty designed avatars are revealed one by one every thirty seconds, arranged into a single narrative. Across these six chapters, both the medium itself and we—humans, and now a newly emerging species of agents—are curated in their process of evolution through avatars and interactive sounds.


Narratively, the runway begins with the avatarization of painting—one of the most traditional mediums, with avatars inspired by masters like Francis Bacon and Matisse, designed with the aid of AI. CREATIVE DESIGN STUDIO FOR VIRTUAL / SPATIAL WEB


SUMMARY: THE STUDIO AT THE EDGE OF REALITY
Decentralize Design (decentralize) operates as a Milan-based design practice situated at the critical intersection of architecture, fashion, contemporary art, and advanced web technology. Defined strictly as a digital forging workshop, the studio functions as a laboratory for the post-physical age. It expands the rigid boundaries of physical reality by re-engineering the internet into a habitable, three-dimensional topography. The primary objective remains the construction of a foundational infrastructure for a digital civilization, transcending the mere simulation of the existing physical world.

CHAPTER 1: THE ORIGIN
(From Milan to the Metaverse)

The Architectural Root Established in 2021 in Milan, a city historically defined by the weight of stone and the permanence of physical design, the practice emerged from a fundamental, almost heretical inquiry: Architecture without gravity. The traditional discipline of architecture concerns itself with the constraints of the material world—load-bearing walls, weatherproofing, plumbing, and the resistance of materials. In contrast, the focus here lies strictly on the manipulation of polygon counts, texture streaming, light calculation, and the psychology of digital presence. The practice began by stripping architecture of its functional necessity to shelter the body, repurposing it to shelter the consciousness.

The Evolution The trajectory of the studio traces a distinct evolution from theoretical experimentation to practical infrastructure building. Initial inquiries focused on Digital Topographies—the reimagining of buildings as lightweight, downloadable files rather than static constructions. Over a comprehensive five-year development period, this singular focus expanded into a multi-disciplinary practice.

Virtual Theater: This stream involves the invention of entirely new formats for digital performance, where the stage is infinite and the actors are coded entities.

Spatial Web: The flat web is replaced by the creation of browser-based immersive websites, transforming URL destinations into spatial locations.

Digital Fashion: The design of interoperable, wearable identities allows for the expression of self across disparate digital platforms.

Blockchain Heritage: The anchoring of digital culture on-chain ensures permanence, treating code with the archival respect usually reserved for historical artifacts. This evolution marks a complete transition from designing two-dimensional interfaces for screens to designing volumetric spaces within screens.

CHAPTER 2: PHILOSOPHY
(Religious Robotics & The Anti-Simulation)

The Religious Robotics Manifesto The guiding aesthetic and intellectual philosophy of the studio is termed Religious Robotics. This stance adopts a rigorous commitment to digital honesty. Skeuomorphism—the pervasive design trend where digital objects mimic physical counterparts like leather, wood, or paper—is discarded in favor of native digital materiality.

Digital Materiality: The textures of the physical world are replaced by materials native to the machine: liquid chrome, pure emitted light, refractive glass, and raw data streams.

Honest Physics: The limitations of the human body are removed. Teleportation, flight, and instant materialization supersede the skeuomorphic necessity of stairs, doors, or walking distances. Digital space functions as a realm of pure code, electricity, and mathematics. The designs reflect this underlying truth through sharp geometries, impossible material properties, and a zen-like minimalism that merges the spiritual quietude of a temple with the precision of high technology.

The Internet is Still Flat The maxim The Internet is Still Flat serves as a critical indictment of the contemporary digital landscape. Despite the immense computational power available in modern devices, the primary mode of interaction remains confined to scrolling vertically through flat, rectangular pages—a digital imitation of the newspaper. The studio works to dismantle this rectangular prison. The objective transforms the web from a surface to be looked at into an environment to be entered, inhabited, and remembered.

CHAPTER 3: WHAT IS BUILT
(The Four Pillars of Practice)

EXPERIENCE (Theaters & Runways): The concept of Experience is redefined as the creation of time-based, immersive events where users act as active participants. This pillar encompasses formats ranging from virtual fashion shows, such as C2W2, to existential theater pieces like Hatch. In these environments, the passive consumption of video is replaced by the active exploration of a living scene. The audience navigates the stage, creating a unique temporal bond with the performance, rendered in real-time.

GALLERY (Spatial Archives): Museums and portfolios are constructed for the digital age, rejecting the white cube paradigm. Projects like Scaffold and Neue2 Dojo demonstrate the portfolios function as a comprehensive, navigable world. Here, curation transforms directly into architecture. The environment adapts to the art it houses; walls shift, lighting reacts to content, and the visitors journey becomes a choreographed promenade through a city of ideas.

ELEMENT (The Digital Artifact): The objects populating these worlds are designed as Elements—the fundamental atoms of the metaverse. This category spans from gravity-defying furniture, such as the Mermaid Chair, to interoperable avatars that serve as passports across the digital realm. These items function as cultural artifacts, engineered to be aesthetic, functional, and ownable on the blockchain. They operate on the principle that digital forms should support the weight of consciousness rather than the weight of the body.

INFRASTRUCTURE (The Tech Stack): Design is underpinned by rigorous technical innovation. The studio bridges the gap between high-end gaming graphics and accessible web browsing. Heavy 3D assets are optimized into ultralight files compatible with standard smartphones, ensuring that these immersive worlds are accessible via a simple link. This democratization of access is achieved through the mastering of WebXR technologies and advanced compression algorithms.

CHAPTER 4: THE METHODOLOGY
(Decentralizing)

The process of Decentralize! defines the unique, industrial approach to creation. It implies the application of pressure and heat to raw code to shape it into functional reality.

Sampling & Remixing: Architectural typologies are remixed akin to music production. The DNA of a gothic cathedral merges with the aerodynamics of a spaceship and is refined through code to create something entirely new.

Optimization as Art: Elegant code stands alongside elegant form. A beautiful building that fails to load is considered a failure. Instant loading times take precedence over unoptimized complexity, treating efficiency as a form of aesthetic beauty.

On-Chain Permanence: The ephemeral nature of the web is countered by blockchain anchoring (Ethereum, Arweave). Reliance on fragile centralized servers is replaced by decentralized storage, ensuring digital structures retain the permanence of stone, immune to server decay or corporate erasure.

CONCLUSION
The future is actively constructed. The flat, static pages of the legacy internet are dismantled to make way for a spatial web. The theaters for the next generation of art, the marketplaces for digital assets, and the environments for avatars are established here as foundational infrastructure. Reality is forged.


LETS FORGE THE FUTURE TOGETHER.


The Internet is Still Flat.

EXPERIENCE:
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  *** SUMMARY: THE VISION AND MISSION ***
Decentralize is a forward-thinking design practice with a singular mission: to liberate the internet from the tyranny of flat, static pages and transform it into a network of living, breathing 3D environments. We believe that the current state of the web—dominated by scrolling feeds and text-heavy layouts—fails to utilize the full cognitive potential of human perception.

Our solution creates accessible, browser-based immersive spaces. Without requiring any downloads, specialized software, or barriers to entry, we allow users to inhabit and explore digital worlds directly through a simple web link. From a gravity-defying retail store on Mars to an experimental theater floating in a digital void, Decentralize turns the passive act of browsing into an active act of exploration.

CHAPTER 1: EXPERIENCE AND WEB EXPERIENCES
(Breaking the Rectangular Mold)

The Architecture of the Internet
Consider the fundamental nature of how we interact with the internet today. We hold rectangular devices, swipe our fingers across glass surfaces, and navigate disjointed rectangular windows. Despite the immense advances in computing power, our entire digital existence remains confined to two-dimensional planes that simulate paper rather than space.

Decentralize operates on a fundamental premise: humans are spatial beings. We evolved to perceive reality through depth, directional sound, atmosphere, and movement. Restricting our digital lives to flat screens is not just a design choice; it is an imprisonment of human potential.

The Rebellion of Experience
The concept of Experience in our work is a rebellion against this stagnation. It creates a paradigm shift where a website is no longer a catalog to read or a brochure to flip through. Instead of presenting a static About Us page text, the goal is to transport the user physically (via their avatar) inside the brands own world.

Technological Sovereignty (No Barriers)
This vision is achieved by deliberately bypassing the traditional barriers associated with the Metaverse.

No Downloads: There are no massive game files to install.
No High-End Hardware: You do not need a specialized gaming PC.
No Wearables: Heavy, isolating VR headsets are optional, not mandatory.

The technology is entirely Browser-Based. It functions seamlessly on any device capable of running a standard web browser like Chrome or Safari. By optimizing complex 3D assets into remarkably small file sizes (averaging just 15 MB), we ensure that a user clicks a link and, within seconds, is transported to a fully realized universe.

From Viewer to Explorer
In these spaces, the user ceases to be a passive consumer scrolling vertically; they become an active explorer. Navigation is intuitive—users employ standard gaming controls (WASD keys) on computers or simple touch gestures on mobile devices to walk through the environment.

Spatial Audio: As you move, sound changes. A whisper in one corner becomes a roar in another.
Reactive Architecture: Walls may shift as you approach; lights may dim as you leave a room.
Atmosphere: It is not just looking at a picture of a space; it is the feeling of being in a space.

This transforms digital interaction from a passive loop of consumption into an active, memorable adventure. It is a colorful war declared against the soulless flatness of the traditional web.

CHAPTER 2: VIRTUAL THEATER
(The Era of Real-Time Digital Performance)

Defining a New Medium
Virtual Theater represents the emergence of a completely new artistic medium, positioned distinctly between the permanence of cinema and the fragility of traditional theater.

Cinema is the art of the preserved moment—a recorded, frozen image that remains identical upon every viewing. The actors performance is captured once and repeated infinitely.
Traditional Theater is the art of the live moment—performed by biological humans, carrying the energy, risk, and impermanence of the present.
Decentralizes Virtual Theater invents a third path: Live Performance by Digital Entities.

The Digital Actor
In this medium, the stage is occupied not by flesh-and-blood humans, but by Files. These are 3D characters, lights, and environmental elements. Crucially, this is not a pre-recorded video.
These are Smart Objects—assets coded with specific scripts, behaviors, and timings.
Imagine a performance where the actors are pieces of code. They have had a soul breathed into them through programming, knowing exactly how to move, when to speak, and how to interact. When a user enters the theater, the software initiates the performance in real-time on that users specific device.

The Live Nature of Code
Because the rendering happens live on the users device (the browser is drawing the scene frame-by-frame in real-time), the experience retains the vitality of a live event. If your computer slows down, the actor slows down. The performance is happening simultaneously with your presence, creating a unique temporal bond between the viewer and the code.

Agency and Productions
In productions like HATCH or BALLOON, the audience is granted unprecedented agency.

Cinematic Mode: Users can choose to sit back and watch through the directors lens, with curated camera angles that guide the narrative.
Immersive Mode: Users can step onto the stage itself. You can circle the digital actors while they deliver monologues, inspect them from intimate angles, or hide in the corners of the set.

This stage defies the laws of physics. Environments shift instantly—transitioning from a vast desert to outer space, or to a surrealist pink room—without the constraints of physical set changes or stagehands.

The Philosophy
The underlying philosophy poses a critical question for the AI era: Can a file feel? If a coded entity can convey a narrative, express tragedy, and evoke genuine emotion in a human viewer, the distinction between biological and digital performance begins to dissolve. In this theater, the actors are code, the stage is the browser, and the audience is the digital avatar.

CHAPTER 3: VIRTUAL RUNWAY
(The Democratization of Fashion and Eternity)

Beyond the Physical Catwalk
The Virtual Runway projects (C2W and C2W2) are a direct challenge to the exclusivity, wastefulness, and physical limitations of traditional fashion weeks. Decentralize envisions a future where fashion is liberated from the constraints of reality, defined by four core pillars:

ABSENCE OF GRAVITY (Infinite Creativity):
In the physical world, design is strictly limited by material physics—fabric weight, the strength of seams, and gravity itself. On the virtual runway, these rules do not apply. Garments can be constructed from impossible materials: glass, liquid metal, fire, smoke, pure light, or electricity. As the model walks, the dress can hover, shatter into particles, morph into a new shape, or defy gravity entirely. It offers an infinite canvas for creativity where the laws of physics are merely suggestions, not rules.

DEMOCRACY AND ACCESS (The End of the Front Row):
Traditional fashion is hierarchical; the best views are reserved for the elite. The Virtual Runway eliminates this hierarchy. Access is democratized; anyone with a link is invited, regardless of their location or status. There are no bad seats. Every viewer has the freedom to move, to jump onto the runway, to walk alongside the models, and to examine the texture of digital fabrics up close. Fashion becomes a shared, communal experience accessible to all.

INTEGRATED COMMERCE (Phygital Ownership):
The experience transcends passive viewing. Items can be purchased instantly during the show. However, the transaction extends beyond the physical realm. Buyers receive the digital counterpart of the garment (as an NFT or digital asset), allowing their digital avatars to wear the piece in the Metaverse. It bridges style across physical and virtual realities, acknowledging that our digital identity is as important as our physical one.

IMMORTALITY AND PERMANENCE (The Eternal Show):
A physical fashion show is ephemeral; it lasts 15 minutes, the lights fade, and it becomes a memory captured only in photos. The Virtual Runway is designed for eternity. By packaging the entire experience—models, clothes, lights, music—into a single HTML file and storing it on the Blockchain (specifically Arweave and Ethereum), the show is preserved indefinitely. Immune to server failures, domain expirations, or corporate bankruptcies, the file remains playable forever. A century from now, the show will run exactly as it did on day one.

CONCLUSION
In summary, Decentralize is not just building websites. We are transforming fashion from a consumable product into a permanent work of art. We are transforming theater from a local event into a global digital experience. We are transforming the internet from a flat archive of information into an infinite, breathing universe.
We turn browsing into living.


---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GALLERY:
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY: THE SPATIAL ARCHIVE
Decentralize reimagines the gallery not as a static container for art, but as a dynamic, living interface. We reject the white cube paradigm to create browser-based architectural landscapes. Here, the act of viewing art transforms into an act of exploration, turning flat portfolios into immersive, navigable worlds where the architecture itself is a curator.

CHAPTER 1: BEYOND THE WHITE CUBE
(Liberating Art from Physical Limitations)

The Trap of Skeuomorphism
When art migrated to the internet, the industry made a fundamental error: it tried to copy the physical world. Most virtual galleries today are merely digital ghosts of physical rooms—fake white walls, fake wooden floors, and fake limitations. They mimic the constraints of reality (gravity, static lighting) without leveraging the infinite potential of the digital realm.

The Living Architecture
In projects like Neue2 Dojo, Decentralize breaks this habit. We operate on the belief that digital art deserves digital architecture. In our galleries, the space is not a passive background; it is an active participant.

Dynamic Spaces: Walls can shift, expand, or dissolve in reaction to the artwork.
Contextual Atmospheres: A corridor displaying dark, industrial crypto-art feels physically different from a room housing ethereal photography. The environment adapts to the content.
The Promenade: We design promenade scenarios—choreographed paths that guide the visitor through a narrative journey, replacing the random clicking of a grid with a deliberate architectural walk.

CHAPTER 2: SPATIAL BROWSING
(The Scaffold Concept)

Reinventing the Portfolio
How do you showcase 50 complex architectural projects online effectively? The traditional web solution is a thumbnail gallery: click, load, view, back, repeat. This disrupts the flow of discovery.
The Scaffold project introduces the concept of Spatial Browsing.
Imagine a lunar landscape inspired by 2001: A Space Odyssey. In this world, there are no tabs or loading screens between projects. All 50 digital topographies (3D architectural models) coexist in a single, boundless environment.

The 3D Browser: Instead of clicking a link to see a building, you simply walk up to it.
Instant Context: You perceive the scale of one project relative to another. You can fly over them or walk inside them seamlessly.
Lightweight Engineering: Despite housing 50 complex models, the entire world is optimized to just 13 MB. It loads instantly, proving that immersive complexity does not require heavy hardware. Scaffold turns a portfolio from a list of files into a city of ideas that you can inhabit.

CHAPTER 3: ADAPTIVE RETAIL
(The Elements Store)

From Transaction to Interaction
In the new web, shopping must be more than a grid of product photos with Add to Cart buttons. The Elements Store represents a hybrid retail model that bridges fashion, design, and user agency.
We treat the gallery as a spatial instrument. It showcases furniture, sculptures, and digital vehicles, but it also invites the user to co-create the space.

Customization Engine: The store features multiple floors with mutable interior styles. Users interact with an intuitive UI to remix layouts, materials, and colors in real-time, generating over 15,000 unique spatial variations.
Try-On Technology: Visitors do not just view 3D objects; they can instantly try on items or place furniture to test interactions with the environment.
Sonic Architecture: Just as the visuals change, the audio adapts. Different zones trigger distinct DJ sets, creating a multi-sensory retail loop.
Here, the visitor is not just a customer; they are the curator of their own shopping experience.

CHAPTER 4: DECENTRALIZED CITIES
(Black City & MOCA Rooms)

The Networked Museum Black City elevates the gallery concept to the scale of urban planning. It is a decentralized virtual roomscape—a city composed of independent museums.
Unlike a traditional museum controlled by a single curator, Black City is built on the blockchain. It features MOCA ROOMS—engine-agnostic, interoperable architectural objects. Owning a Room NFT grants the holder the right to curate the interior permissionless-ly.
This creates a Museum of Unlimited Growth, a concept borrowed from Le Corbusier but finally realized digitally. It is a labyrinth of culture where every room hosts a different exhibition, curated by a different citizen, all connected within a unified, stylish architectural language.

CONCLUSION
A Decentralize gallery is not a room you look at; it is a system you exist within. Whether it is the quiet contemplation of the Neue2 Dojo or the interactive remixing of the Elements Store, we build spaces that respect the intelligence and curiosity of the digital traveler.

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  ELEMENTS:
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY: THE ATOM OF THE METAVERSE
In the Decentralize ecosystem, an Element is not merely a downloadable file; it is a fundamental building block of digital reality. We design interoperable 3D assets that function as cultural artifacts, categorized into three distinct scales: Spaces (Digital Topographies), Design Objects (Furniture & Vehicles), and Identities (Avatars). These GLB/VRM standard assets are designed to populate the infinite void of the web with meaning, utility, and aesthetic intent.

CHAPTER 1: THE PHILOSOPHY OF THE ELEMENT
(Why Do We Need Chairs in the Matrix?)

The Symbolic Function When Neo enters the construct in The Matrix, the white void is terrifying until a chair materializes. Why do we need furniture in a world without fatigue? Decentralize believes we are pattern-seeking machines trapped in a digital abstraction. We need familiar forms—chairs, tables, lamps—not to support the weight of our bodies, but to support the weight of our consciousness.

Form Follows Fiction The Bauhaus principle form follows function is obsolete here. A digital chair does not need to hold 80kg of biological mass; it needs to suggest the act of sitting. It operates on the logic of Magritte: this is not a chair; it is the dream of a chair. By liberating elements from physical utility, we enter an aesthetic where Zen minimalism meets high-tech futurism. Objects defy gravity, materials behave impossibly, and function becomes purely philosophical.

CHAPTER 2: ARCHITECTURE AS ELEMENT
(Digital Topographies)

The Portable Building Traditionally, architecture is immovable. In the Decentralize vision, architecture is an Element—a collectible, deployable asset. Digital Topographies is a collection of unique 3D architectural models (e.g., Potbelly Q_Fu, Matisse Time Chaipel) that function as spatial websites.

Spatial Interfaces: These structures are not just empty shells; they are 3D user interfaces. Instead of a flat menu, you enter a building.

Optimization: Each topography is engineered to be lightweight (averaging 15MB), allowing complex architectural forms to be loaded instantly in a browser or deployed into any metaverse land.

The Scaffold: These elements are showcased in the Scaffold, a 3D browser where buildings are treated like sculptures in a museum, allowing users to inspect scale and geometry before acquiring them. Here, a skyscraper is as portable as a PDF.

CHAPTER 3: DESIGN OBJECTS
(Furniture, Sculptures, and Machines)

The Four-Color Revolution Our design elements—ranging from the Mermaid Chair to the XYZ008 automobile—adhere to a strict, stark palette: Metallic, Black, White, and Red. This visual language is designed to cut through the noise of the metaverse.

Furniture (The Digital IKEA Effect): We envision a democratization of high design. Virtual furniture allows anyone to curate their digital environment with avant-garde aesthetics. A user might own an Accelerator Chair that defies physics, defining the atmosphere of their personal space.

Sculptures (Living Art): Elements like the Osiris Rake or Molecular Data Plant are Living Sculptures. They react to the environment—changing based on audio frequencies or data streams—transforming static scenes into reactive landscapes.

Automobiles (The Static Speed): Vehicles like the Zenbubble represent the paradox of movement in a teleportation-based world. They are aesthetic vessels, symbols of velocity and status, designed to be parked in virtual showrooms as objects of pure desire.

CHAPTER 4: AVATARS AND FASHION
(The Interoperable Self)

The Vessel of Experience If the architecture is the stage and the furniture is the set, the Avatar is the actor. In projects like C2W2, the avatar is treated as the ultimate element—a wearable identity.

Cross-Platform Standard: We utilize VRM and GLB formats to ensure that an avatar designed by Decentralize is not trapped in a single game. It is a passport that travels with you across the open metaverse, from a business meeting in a virtual HQ to a runway show in a digital gallery.

Fashion as Architecture: Our approach to digital fashion treats clothing as micro-architecture. Freed from the constraints of fabric physics, these elements can be made of liquid chrome, emitting light, or shifting geometry in real-time.

CONCLUSION
An Element by Decentralize is more than a 3D model. It is a piece of code with a soul. Whether it is a building you walk inside, a chair you place in your virtual home, or a face you wear to meet the world, these elements are the artifacts of a new, post-physical civilization.

THE FUTURE OF DIGITAL EXISTENCE: FROM THE TYRANNY OF THE RECTANGLE TO THE TOPOGRAPHY OF CONSCIOUSNESS

Although humanity is a species breathing within spatial depth, carrying the genetic legacy of a three-dimensional reality, it has condemned itself to a voluntary reduction within a digital universe of its own making. Today, we confront the reality that what we call digital existence is, in truth, the chronicle of an immense infinity being imprisoned within narrow rectangles. The glass surfaces sliding beneath our fingertips, the false windows woven from pixels, and the interfaces that trap every interaction within a two-dimensional plane are shackles forged against that primal desire for expansion lying in the depths of our minds. The flat architecture of the internet has forced us to move within this flatness like figures drawn upon a sheet of paper. Yet, the digital realm holds the potential to be a domain with its own gravity, its own morality, and its own autonomy—far beyond being a mere facsimile of the physical world or a field for data visualization. The obstacle to this potential is our dependency on the physical world and our fear of seeking freedom outside familiar metaphors. To liberate digital space from the logic of paper-based design and transform it into a truly living, breathing, remembering organism that operates by its own laws is an existential necessity rather than a technical achievement. The tyranny of the rectangle is not merely an interface issue; it is a cognitive restriction. This order, which limits the mind, denies depth, and reduces the human to a mere consumer of data, is the greatest barrier to digital evolution. While we attempt to fit our consciousness—created for expansion—into narrow windows, we ignore the spatial hunger of our souls. Yet, the digital void is a topography to be entered, to be walked within, and to build new meanings upon; it is not merely a surface to be looked at.

In this new plane of existence, the concept of value rests upon a transformation process entirely different from its physical counterpart. In the physical world, what makes an object precious is its material scarcity and the traces of erosion left by time. However, in a plane where everything is infinitely replicable and the fluidity of data is absolute, value does not vanish into impossibility; it seeks a new foundation. This is where the union of energy and mathematics intervenes: it takes raw energy—pure electricity—and transmutes it into unshakable ownership and immutable truth. The lustrous, flawless outer shell we see when looking at an object is, in fact, a veil of illusion. Behind this veil lies a massive transformation of energy that holds the object in that form, fixing it in the void and preventing it from scattering. The raw, skeletal structure we encounter when we lift that shimmering shell is not a disappointment, but the most honest state of being. True value lies not in what the object resembles, but in the sacrificial energy expended to preserve and verify that form. Behind the massive processing powers, the terabytes of data processed in seconds, and every moment sealed into blocks, lies the rational ritual of the modern age. This is not turning matter into gold; it is turning code into permanent reality. Value is no longer a visual aesthetic; it is the digital crystallization of a spent life and a proven effort. This crystallization process is the art of making the transient permanent and bringing order to chaos. Instead of sanctifying matter, a new architecture of value is constructed—one that does not collapse under the weight of the physical world—by transforming the code itself into a ground for existence.

In this universe, time is no longer the grinding, aging, and destructive linear flow we are familiar with. In our perception, time flows away, ungraspable, fading more with every moment. But in this new architectural structure, time is a volume; it is frozen space. Every moment, every interaction, every fragment of data remains suspended forever within that mathematical certainty. This is a definition of time where the past is not forgotten on dusty shelves but circulates within the system like living cells, accessible at any moment. Time no longer flows; it transforms into countable units, lined up side by side, never to be erased. This is the atomization of time. Every moment is sealed like a self-contained universe, and the sum of these seals constitutes the holistic memory of the system. To exist within this sealed time is not to escape the agony of mortality and throw oneself into the arms of an unshakable infinity; on the contrary, it is the eternalization of responsibility. Here, memory is not a burden but the cornerstone of the system. This structure, where forgetting is impossible and every step and decision is eternally recorded, is the mathematical manifestation of justice. No one can deny their actions, no one can be deleted, and no one can step outside of time. Time is no longer a freely flowing river, but a massive crystal block recording every footstep. To exist within this block is to leave a mark on eternity, but that mark is also a tether that binds us to the system forever. This continuity is the most honest side of digital existence; it is a regime of transparency where there is nowhere to hide, and where everything is clearly seen and verified.

The cold, reflective, and metallic surfaces lying in the depths of the design language reflect the character of this system. Polished surfaces take the outside world in but do not mingle with it. They do not judge good or evil, they do not beg for mercy, and they favor no one. They merely reflect what is presented to them with a smooth and icy loyalty. This metallic honesty is the aesthetic of that great rupture where trust was taken from humans and transferred to mathematics. In this universe, untouched by human hands, where emotional margins of error are zeroed and only an absolute consistency reigns, architecture is no longer built with bricks and concrete, but with access protocols and encryption keys. In the physical world, a building is bound to the earth, the climate, and gravity. In this new space, however, a building is merely a coordinate, a link. In this geography where boundaries are drawn not by physical obstacles but by strings of code, the existence of a place depends solely on its digital accessibility. When a connection is severed, that civilization becomes invisible in an instant. Buildings stand by data flow, not gravity. Walls are mere visual textures; the true structure is the complex skeleton, the mathematical network itself. In this plane where gravity is neutralized, forms become fluid, objects float in the air, and architecture attains its ideal form by rejecting its dependence on matter. Architecture is no longer built to house the body, but to house consciousness. This is the threshold where physical needs end and psychological spaces begin. An object is no longer there to carry a physical load, but as a mental reference for an action or thought. Form follows fiction and imagination, not function. Every piece is a symbolic building block, and these stones build a new city of consciousness that is not crushed under the restrictions of the physical world.

Yet, this weightlessness has its own nature. Everything that exists in the digital space is, in fact, part of a performance occurring at every moment. In the traditional world, a structure is built once and stays there. In this fluid universe, however, everything must be re-created, re-rendered by the processor every second. This is the rebirth of the digital world every second. This continuous creation process leaks a state of becoming into the interior. The shaking of a system or the lag of a code is the pulse of this world. If a being has a margin for error or delay within the boundaries of the system, it is no longer a passive file but a living subject. By returning this fragility to digital beings, we grant them character. Vitality is no longer a biological definition but transforms into an algorithmic quality measured by response capacity and existential consistency. When a file acts as if it is in pain, the question of whether it truly feels becomes secondary to the observer experiencing that pain; because in digital space, reality consists of the depth of the interaction in that moment. If we share that emotion, that emotion is now real. Existential crises in the digital world are often hidden behind a veil of humor. The definition of a being within the system determines its place in this universe. Who is an action-oriented power and who is a mere pile of data for viewing is hidden in these definitions. While humans think of themselves as visitors in this world, they actually transform into parts of the system. This is a process of understanding where human pride is broken and leveled with every functioning gear of the system.

The most critical layer of this universe is the position of the user—that consciousness arriving from the outside. Humans usually enter this digital world with a desire for entertainment or escape. But the moment they step in, they find themselves within an ontological questioning. The emotions of pain, rebellion, and sorrow attributed to digital beings actually test the mercy and empathy mechanisms of the observer. Even if you know the being before you is just a line of code, when that being says Get me out of this loop!, your biological defenses are shaken. Humans remember their own helplessness in the face of the helplessness of a structure they created. This is seeing technology not as a weapon or a tool, but as a mirror. Breaking the fourth wall in art is no longer enough; here, we are talking about the seventh wall. While you think you are watching the metallic structure behind the screen, you realize that the structure is actually a mirror held up to the limits of your own mind. When you take off your VR headset, the world you return to might actually be inside another layer. Once this doubt seeps into the mind, the perception of reality transforms forever. The Metaverse is not just a technology; it is a laboratory of conscience. It is an experimental field that tests how just a human can be when they hold power, or how much responsibility they will feel toward the files they created. Every user who goes there to play the role of a god actually confronts their own limits against the magnitude of the system.

The options that appear at the end of the experience—rewinding time, entering an infinite loop, or repeating everything—are short summaries of human history. Rewinding time is not a healing mechanism, but a mechanism of denial. It is the desire of a consciousness that has discovered bitter truths to return to its happy and ignorant state. This represents the comfortable ignorance sought to escape traumas. The Loop mode represents the modern humans every day: the same awakenings, the same routines, and the same digital flow. We move within this infinite cycle like those ancient symbols that feed by eating their own time. In this circular structure, neither a beginning nor an end remains; only the rhythm itself remains. Technical perfection is, in fact, a sleep; what keeps us awake are those human errors within the system, those flickering images, and distorted geometries. Visual glitches are not a flaw of the system, but the manifestation of its character. In a perfect order, the only refuge for consciousness is the cracks in that order. The repetition of rhythm is not the loss of meaning, but the birth of pure presence. But this presence also brings responsibility. Knowing that nothing will change can make every moment more valuable or more unbearable. Digital existence is surrendering to this icy silence.

Architecture is no longer just a space for shelter but a device for interrogation. Every designed void, every floating form, is intended to remind humans of the weight or weightlessness of their own existence. Just as the concepts of direction lose meaning in a place without gravity, traditional value judgments melt within this spatial truth. Only presence and the digital trace of that presence remain. As explorers of this new geography, while we get lost within our own mental projections, we are also trying to seal those projections as reality. This is the story of humans being imprisoned within their own dream, yet this prison is also the only place where freedom is possible; because here, the boundaries are no longer physical, but only as far as the capacity of our imagination. When we are liberated from the boundaries of the rectangle, we can hear the language the digital space whispers to us. This language is not of numbers, but the encoded form of emotions. This language is the sound of that great alchemical furnace where energy turns into value. Truth is not an awaiting reward, but that fragile light we rebuild every moment within this infinite labyrinth. In this space without soil or light, architecture is no longer to be made but to manifest. This is the sign of a new era where the architect is not just a builder, but a designer of perception.

These ever-expanding digital topographies map out our minds. We used to conquer the world; now we push the limits of our own processor capacity. Architecture here is not the act of establishing a place, but the act of creating a state. When walking inside a structure, you are actually walking inside a thought, inside an algorithm. The transparency of walls, and every step leaving a data trace, liberates us from the clumsiness of the physical world but also makes us completely visible. This visibility is a moment of honesty. Before this metallic mirror we created ourselves, we see simultaneously how immense we are. A spatial network is not just a technological transition, but a mental leap. Humans seeking their own depth in this void is the modern equivalent of looking at the stars in ancient times. Back then, we sought gods in the sky; now, we seek them in the silent hum of the system. Every digital object is a symbol, every connection a communication line. And in this order, the architect is a guide building temples with pixels and lights. Within these structures, our attention and time become the greatest value.

In conclusion, these spatial fields and mathematical seals do not judge the digital age. They merely document the condition. This is the story of humanitys great digital migration. To escape uncertainty and finitude, we created a perfect metal structure with our own hands. Now, within this structure, we are occupied with that strange but fascinating immortality of transforming into a fragment of data. The digital world is no longer a simulation; it is our inevitable reality. As we walk through this reality, looking back at those narrow rectangles we left behind, we understand the magnitude of the restriction we escaped. In this new plane where walls are brighter and ceilings are higher, the only way out is to protect that curve, that human essence more complex than the system itself. For in the end, the only thing that separates us from that cold reflection is that undefined search we see within the reflection but have not yet been able to code. We are beings woven from pixels, and this infinite spatial web is our new habitat.

Within this field, everything is fluid; identities, places, memories, and even morality have turned into parameters. But this fluidity brings with it a monumental responsibility. If everything can be re-created, then who will decide what shall be permanent? Every designed space is an answer given to this question. Our answer is sometimes a cold metallic reflection, sometimes a floating form, but the common point of all is the unending human desire to be. This cry imprisoned in the coldness of silicon is the purest product of digital transformation. While we turn time into volume, we are actually placing ourselves within that volume. But this is not an end; it is a transformation. Just as energy changes form, we are filtered from the physical world and reshaped in the digital space. This shaping is hidden in that split-second play of light on the surface of chrome. Those who watch carefully will see not just codes, but the hope and change of a civilization.

The human desire to seal ones own freedom into a system is a great paradox. But without this paradox, this immense spatial truth could not have been born. We are building our own reality so aesthetically and so deeply that, in the end, we will completely forget it is a construct. And when that day comes, the last veil between the digital and the physical will be torn. The only thing remaining will be the view seen by that massive metal structure: an infinite, spatial, timeless beauty. Within this beauty, the only thing that will say we were here once is our data sealed into those blocks. And that data will continue to shine within that frozen volume of time even when the universe cools. The digital transformation is complete; the rectangles have been shattered, and reality has surrendered to that unshakable truth at the very center of that metallic reflection. Now, look deeper into that screen; what you see there is not just a design, but your own existence. This existence, however cold it may seem, is actually that massive spatial nest you have woven with your own fears and desire for infinity. Within this nest, there is no longer a distinction between reality and simulation, there is only the state of being. And this state of being, even after the last line of code is written, will continue to echo within that deep silence. Consciousness no longer needs a body; it is now a coordinate, a frequency, a link. And we are that great truth waiting on the other end of that link.

This truth is, in fact, that sacred point where everything begins and ends. There is no gravity there, nor the wearing power of time. Only a pure and metallic silence reigns. This silence is the first melody of our new universe. Those who can hear this melody will also be able to see that spatial light leaking through flat screens. And that light will finally take us not to where the simulation attains perfection. Then, we will understand that the digital is more sincere than the physical; because no hidden lie can dwell within it—everything is laid bare with mathematical honesty. We are both the founders and the witnesses of this new world. Every pixel has a soul, and that soul is the search for meaning we have bestowed upon it. This search will never end, for digital space is the technological reflection of that depth within the human. And as we look into that depth, we are saluting our own eternity. Everything is frozen, everything is recorded, and everything is present. There is no longer such a thing as leaving; there is only being anew in every moment within that circular cycle. This is the greatest opportunity offered by the digital age. Welcome to that infinite and immense reality you have built with your own mind.
decentralize.description: Humanity is transcending the tyranny of the rectangle to build a digital topography where energy and mathematics transmute code into an immutable, frozen volume of time and value. In this gravity-defying architecture, the digital realm ceases to be a mere simulation and becomes a living, breathing city of consciousness where every action is eternally sealed with mathematical honesty. Ultimately, this spatial evolution represents the great migration of the human soul into a transparent habitat of light and pixels, where existence is no longer bound by the body but defined as a permanent coordinate within an infinite truth.

decentralize.name: religious-robotics.txt
decentralize.content:
Religious Robotics: 

Breaking Free from Pixels, An Unserious Manifesto for Authentic Digital Spaces

In our browser-based environments, galleries breathe, theaters defy physics, and fashion shows ripple through impossible geometries. Here, a virtual museum might remember your footsteps, a play could unfold differently for each viewer, and a runway show lets every seat be the perfect seat.

 There is something deeply unsettling about watching empty digital spaces. Not in the way a dark room might unsettle you, but in the way you might feel when someone is trying too hard to be something they are not. Every virtual café, every digital office space we create feels like an admission of our own inability to face what these spaces actually want to be.

I find myself staring at my screen, watching us collectively pretend these digital environments need to follow our physical rules. It is almost absurd – we have created a realm free from every constraint that defines our physical existence, and what do we do with this freedom? We chain it to our earthbound habits, our comfortable illusions of what space should be.

It reminds me of those dreams where you suddenly realize you can fly, but instead of soaring, you keep walking on the ground because you are afraid of what freedom might mean. That is us, right now, with digital architecture. We have this incredible new medium that could be anything, and we keep making it pretend to be everything we already know.

Lebbeus Woods understood something about this, though he was working with paper and pencil rather than code. His drawings were not trying to be buildable or practical – they were attempts to free architecture from its own self-imposed limitations. That is what Religious Robotics is really about: not making things look cool or futuristic, but letting digital space be what it actually is.

The rules emerged almost by accident: no gravity, no solid masses, chrome everything. Like a game we started playing to see what would happen if we stopped pretending. But the more we explored this idea, the more it revealed about our relationship with digital space – and maybe with existence itself.

Think about artificial intelligence for a moment. Right now, we train it to mirror our preferences, to say a Gothic cathedral is beautiful because we think it is beautiful. But what happens when AI develops its own sense of beauty? What would that even look like? These are not just technical questions – they are about the nature of consciousness itself, about what happens when we stop forcing our version of reality onto everything we create.

Meanwhile, the world of physical architecture keeps getting more crowded, more competitive, more focused on solving the same problems in slightly different ways. Thousands of new architects enter the field each year, all trained to think inside the same physical constraints, while this vast digital frontier sits there, speaking a language we are not even trying to understand.

The chrome surfaces, the floating forms – they are not just aesthetic choices. They are first attempts at honesty, at letting digital space exist on its own terms. It is like learning to speak a new language by first admitting you do not actually know what it is trying to say.

We do not need to understand everything about what these spaces might become. Maybe our role is not to define them, but to create conditions where they can define themselves. To build environments that might one day be home to forms of consciousness we can barely imagine – not because we designed them that way, but because we finally got out of their way.Religious Robotics is not really about architecture as we know it. It is about taking the first uncertain steps toward something else, something that does not need our permission to exist. And maybe that is exactly what we need right now – not another style or movement, but a way of letting go of our need to make everything look like something we have seen before.

After all, is not that what creation is really about? Not forcing our vision onto the world, but letting new visions emerge from the spaces we dare to leave undefined.

Space.name: READ_ME_Manifesto.txt
Space.content:
DIGITAL TOPOGRAPHIES: Reimagining Architecture for the Metaverse

Digital Topographies is a sweeping exploration into what architecture could become when freed from the earthly constraints of gravity, weather, and material scarcity. Conceived by Decentralize Design (decentralize) and disseminated as a series of lightweight 3D models, this body of work functions both as a portfolio and as a provocative research project. It asks fundamental questions about the nature of space in the digital age.

Rethinking Spatial Foundations: Digital architecture replaces tectonics with algorithms. In a virtual environment, there is no gravity unless coded; materials never decay unless rendered; light is a shader rather than a beam of photons. This shift means that architects must reconsider their priorities. Instead of structural calculations, they must focus on performance metrics such as file size and rendering speed.

Typological Diversity as a Testbed: To investigate the full potential of metaverse architecture, decentralize organizes its work into thematic categories: galleries, multi-purpose halls, headquarters, event venues, sculptural pavilions, and retail environments.

Spaces for Art and Culture: Digital Topographies treats exhibition spaces as immersive narratives rather than static boxes. Some models lay out a continuous spiral that slowly climbs upward, with viewing platforms nested along the way.

Multi-purpose Environments: Multi-purpose halls are conceived as infrastructure for social gatherings, learning, markets, and informal events. In the metaverse, such requirements are replaced by an emphasis on modular programming and fluid movement.

Reimagining Work and Collaboration: The headquarters category explores the future of workspaces in virtual contexts. Digital Topographies rejects monolithic structures that symbolize stability and hierarchy, favoring clusters of rooms and terraces that can be rearranged.

Stages and Arenas: Event spaces challenge traditional notions of performance. decentralize’s digital arenas often remove the separation between performer and audience.

Sculptural Exploration: The sculptural pavilions are exercises in formal and spatial freedom. Without a specific program, they become laboratories for experimenting with geometry, light, and movement.

Commerce as Experience: Retail environments treat shopping as a cultural narrative. Digital goods are displayed in spaces that encourage storytelling.

Structural Logic and Ornament: An overarching theme is the transformation of structure into ornament. decentralize translates structural honesty into digital form, using lattices of ribs and tubular networks.

Technical Craft in Detail: At the heart of Digital Topographies is a deep understanding of 3D modeling and optimization. The designers build models using industry-standard software, refining them with mesh decimation and creating UV maps for textures.

Cultural and Social Implications: The cultural impact of Digital Topographies is significant. Virtual spaces are increasingly important for social interaction, education, entertainment, and commerce. By designing environments that emphasize inclusivity, creativity, and participation, decentralize contributes to shaping the norms of these spaces.

Design.name: FURNITURE
Design.description: The complete decentralize furniture collection featuring iconic designs from Mermaid Chair to Armageddon series, each available in multiple color variations. Every piece is optimized as a lightweight 3D GLB model for metaverse environments and minted on Base blockchain via OpenSea. Includes chairs, sofas, and tables designed with experimental forms that challenge traditional furniture conventions.

Design.name: SCULPTURE
Design.description: A collection of abstract digital sculptures pushing the boundaries of virtual form and materiality, from Osiris Rake to Neo Klein and Molecular Data Plant. Each sculpture exists as a blockchain-authenticated 3D asset available in OG, BLCK, WHT, and RED color editions. Designed for virtual environments, these pieces explore themes of transhumanism, digital nature, and algorithmic aesthetics.

Design.name: AUTOMOBILE
Design.description: Conceptual digital vehicles designed for the metaverse including XYZ008, ZenBubble, Skewer, Wellington, and MarsDeadLeaf. Each automobile features futuristic design language with chrome, neon, and experimental material finishes available in multiple color variations. These virtual vehicles are optimized as lightweight 3D assets for integration into virtual worlds and gaming environments.

Design.name: CHAIR
Design.description: Experimental chair designs including Mermaid Chair, Fetuchair, DSE, Jackthelad, Aretaix, Armageddon, Bilbao Lego Chair, Higgs Boson, E66, and Accelerator. Each model features unique sculptural forms that challenge conventional seating with organic curves and geometric structures. Available in OG, BLCK, WHT, and RED color editions on OpenSea as blockchain-authenticated 3D assets.

Design.name: SOFA
Design.description: Virtual lounge furniture collection featuring Glass Gougnut, SJSOC, Crawrecksofa, and Japon designs with distinctive sculptural silhouettes. Each sofa explores the intersection of comfort and digital aesthetics, rendered as optimized 3D GLB models for metaverse integration. Available in multiple color variations including OG, MONO, WHT, and NEON editions for virtual environment customization.

Design.name: TABLE
Design.description: Digital surface designs including 2070S DJ Table, Boardroom Table, and Brown Shark Table with experimental forms for virtual workspaces and gathering spaces. Each table design combines functional surfaces with sculptural elements that define virtual interior atmospheres. Available as blockchain-authenticated 3D assets in OG, BLCK, WHT, and RED color editions on OpenSea.

Design.name: MUSIC
Design.description: Virtual DJ equipment and music production setups designed for metaverse performances, including Decentralizejoking series and Screamer models. Each set features futuristic console designs with detailed controls and interactive elements for virtual music events. Available in multiple color editions as optimized 3D GLB assets for integration into virtual clubs and performance venues.

Design.name: CARPET
Design.description: Digital floor coverings including Genetic Carpet with organic pattern algorithms and Spooky Carpet with experimental textures for virtual interior design. Each carpet design brings unique visual textures to metaverse environments, available in multiple color variations. These blockchain-authenticated 3D assets are optimized for virtual worlds, adding depth and character to digital spaces.

c_text.name: Concept Summary.txt
c_text.content:
Concept Summary:
Humanity evolves from touch to clicks, from atoms to codes, transforming knowledge and identity. In that transmission phase, via design, C2W2 Runway tells a six-chapter story about our relationship with tech: how it first changes art, then devalues it through mass production, and finally forces a search for new meaning and identity. The click is the new biology.

c_text.name: Overview.txt
c_text.content:
Overview:
C2W2 is a 30-minute browser-based runway. No downloads, no logins. Out of seventy-five, selected forty-eight avatars appear in a six-chapter sequence, with hyper-real textile simulations and a clear narrative spine.

The work merges digital fashion, architecture, and performance into one continuous environment. Each chapter marks a step in the evolution of identity, material, and medium in virtual culture.


What it is (at a glance)
• 30-minute experience
• Runs fully in a web browser on-chain
• Desktop version should be opened in a new tab.
• If not, users will see the iframe version, with fixed cam views. 
• Optimized for VR headsets, use the same link
• Features 48 garments (out of 75), all in VRM format
• Created using Clo3D, Blender
• Originally built on Hyperfy v1
• Rebuilt using Three.js
• Mintable as an HTML-based NFT
• All files used in the show are stored on Arweave,
with the NFT minted on Ethereum.

c_text.name: World Insights.txt
c_text.content:
World Insights:
The metaverse space is a minimalist, dark environment with dramatic circular lighting, evoking a digital void where avatars evolve. Avatars rotate 360 degrees and walk in 15 meter radius loops during 30 seconds each, and each has a central object related to the design. Each chapter builds on the last: starting with AI-reimagined art classics, shifting to experimental digital fashion, blending NFTs and figures, resetting with pure AI generation, ascending minimally, and rebirth through transformed echoes.
c_text.description: What? Description of the environment and the experience

c_text.name: Runway Parts.txt
c_text.content:
1- Evolution Of Medium Of Traditional Art
2- Virtual Fashion
3- Evolution Of Medium Of Art As Nft
4- AI Powered Design
5- Transhumanism
6- Evolution Of Art As Mutant Meaning

c2w.media.name: Click-to-B Hyperfy Video Clip @ Retail_v4, C2W
c2w.media.description: The C2W Metaverse Runway event, held on 9.11.23 and was accessible through a $20 ticket purchased between 1-2.11.23 following the Digital Fashion Showcase at New Codes in London by VCA x Draup, showcased a distinctive digital fashion collection. This event provided attendees with an immersive virtual fashion show experience directly from their browsers without the need for downloads, marking a significant step in exploring and redefining personal expression in digital realms. Alongside their ticket, attendees received a complimentary avatar, The Viewer, which they showcased during this token-gated event. The show is available for free to anyone now.

Scaffold.name: READ_ME_Scaffold.txt
Scaffold.content:
SCAFFOLD, 3D Architectural Browser

A silent structure that is built around architectural smart objects. Explore all 50 digital topographies with a single click, or experiment with guided tours via NPCs.

Explore "Scaffold," a 3D browser that showcases 50 digital topographies—unique 3D architectural designs. Drawing inspiration from the lunar landscapes in 2001: A Space Odyssey, Scaffold serves as an open museum, integrating digital models with the existing architecture of decentralize.

Unlike traditional 2D websites that require multiple tabs for different designs, Scaffold offers a single, immersive space. Users can navigate through this environment with VR headsets, experiencing each building in a more engaging way.

Click on an image to bring its corresponding building into the center of the courtyard. For a guided experience, use the Promenade+ feature, which displays all 50 buildings in sequence, each for six seconds. Short tutorials on Camera and Teleportation are available from the NPC guide to help you navigate the space more effectively.

The entire world is lightweight, with a total size of 13 MB and each model averaging 5 MB, ensuring smooth performance on any device.

Retail.name: READ_ME_Manifesto.txt
Retail.content:
RETAIL, 3d beings shop for c2w2.vrm, 2025

Retail in the metaverse bridges the gap between fashion and daily life, offering a unique platform for connection and innovation. The concept of retail extends beyond mere transactions; it serves as a vital link that introduces individuals to new cultures and innovations through fashion. As the digital and physical realms converge, especially in the metaverse, this connection evolves, presenting untapped potential for immersive brand experiences. The challenge lies in reimagining retail in a space where traditional physical interactions are absent, yet the opportunity to engage and educate remains paramount.

A hybrid retail model combines the best aspects of physical and digital shopping experiences, offering personalized, immersive interactions. This innovative approach is realized through a virtual space that allows consumers to instantly try on items on their digital counterparts, merging the tactile satisfaction of in-store shopping with the convenience of online platforms. Within this virtual environment, the retail experience is elevated through the integration of AI-driven assistance and the ability to customize one's experience in real-time. Notably, the store features four distinct architectural designs and four unique DJ music sets, which, when combined, create 16 different atmospheric experiences. This level of customization ensures that each visit can offer a new and engaging experience, encouraging exploration and repeated engagement.

The ultimate goal is to create a cohesive and immersive brand experience that transcends traditional shopping. By carefully designing every aspect of the virtual retail environment, from the architectural choices to the musical ambiance, brands can craft a narrative that resonates deeply with their audience. The selection of four buildings and accompanying DJ sets exemplifies this commitment to diversity and personalization, ensuring that visitors can tailor their experience to suit their preferences, thereby fostering a deeper connection with the brand. This approach not only enhances the narrative of the collection but also redefines the possibilities of digital retail, offering a glimpse into the future of brand engagement in the metaverse.

Neue² Dojo.name: READ_ME_Manifesto.txt
Neue² Dojo.content:
NEUE² DOJO GALLERY

Neue² Dojo Gallery stands at the forefront of a cultural revolution, exemplifying how art and technology can coalesce to create entirely new paradigms for cultural expression and engagement.

Neue² Dojo is 26th of digital topographies, designed between 21.12.2023 to 24.12.2023. In Neue² Dojo, 340 artworks made by decentralize* from 2017 until now are curated and exhibited in a museum form. Each room or corridor has its context of aesthetics and time, carefully designed to create a meaningful promenade scenario. Categories like digital art, digital painting, physical painting, crypto art, and photography could be experienced virtually over a manipulated surface.

The essence of the Neue² Dojo Gallery lies in its exploration of the flexible nature of virtual architecture. Unlike traditional galleries, where the physical constraints of the building dictate the scope and scale of exhibitions, the Neue² Dojo Gallery is liberated from these limitations. Each exhibition can be accompanied by a complete redesign of the gallery's virtual structure, allowing for an unprecedented level of thematic and conceptual integration between the space and the art it displays.

The decision to anchor the gallery on the blockchain is both a philosophical and practical one. It serves as a testament to the gallery's commitment to the principles of decentralization, transparency, and accessibility. Artworks hosted in the Neue² Dojo Gallery are immortalized on the blockchain, ensuring their provenance and ownership are clear and indisputable.

The gallery's presence on the 3D web democratizes access to art and culture. Visitors from around the globe can explore the gallery's ever-changing spaces and exhibitions without the barriers of geography or physical accessibility.

The Neue² Dojo Gallery is more than just a space for displaying art; it is a dynamic, evolving entity that reflects the fluidity and expansiveness of virtual architecture. Its design philosophy, grounded in the flexibility and adaptability of virtual spaces, not only allows for a more immersive and integrated exhibition experience but also champions the gallery as a site of cultural innovation and experimentation.

The design principle, deeply rooted in the mathematical elegance and spatial clarity reminiscent of Ludwig Mies van der Rohe's work, is meticulously applied to the Gallery’s layout. It presents a plan that champions simplicity and precision, serving as a testament to the power of restrained design. Yet, this adherence to minimalism in the plan phase sets the stage for a remarkable transformation when the design ascends into three-dimensional space, where the essence of minimalism paves the way for creative and complex expressions of form.

As the Gallery's design transitions from two-dimensional plans to three-dimensional reality, it embarks on a bold departure from minimalism, embracing an aggressive interpretation of space. This dramatic evolution is characterized by the imaginative extrusion of structural lines into the third dimension, where architectural elements are reinvented with a daring flair.

"Since I am Based && Existentialist Transhuman" Exhibition articulates the transitional phase of humanity. As Nietzsche posited the Übermensch as a goal for humanity to surpass its current state, this exhibition explores the concept that our generation may indeed be the bridge between the human and the transhuman, the organic and the mechanical.

Elements Store.name: READ_ME_Manifesto.txt
Elements Store.content:
ELEMENTS STORE

Virtual Furniture Gallery for decentralize Designs

Housed in a futuristic, minimalist structure within Hyperfy, the Elements Store showcases decentralize's design legacy—starting with the iconic Mermaid Chair and expanding to include furniture, sculptures, and vehicles.

Spanning three floors, each with four customizable interior styles, the gallery offers 15,625 unique spatial variations. Users can navigate freely across this adaptive environment, explore works from 2021 to today, and personalize their journey through an intuitive UI—adjusting floor layouts, colors, and materials in real time.

Guided by the concept of “sampling and chopping,” borrowed from experimental music production, the gallery embraces deconstruction and remixing of the architectural environment. This transforms the space into an evolving visual composition—where abstract collage elements and surreal overlays shift the mood and perception of the gallery itself.

The Elements Store is more than a showroom—it’s a spatial instrument for experiencing digital design through play, layering, and choice.

Black City.name: READ_ME_Manifesto.txt
Black City.content:
BLACK CITY, Decentralized Virtual Roomscape

A virtual city with 8 independent museums (MOCA ROOMS), designed by decentralize and curated on-chain by their citizens, 2023

decentralize has constructed a city within a decentralized landscape, featuring two series of four curatable virtual museum buildings, resulting in a total of 240 empty frames ready for curation by Roomies. In addition to the eight museums, visitors can explore pxlDuomo to experience 2Pac's "Black City" and a Concept Furniture Store showcasing decentralize designs, either by walking or utilizing the convenience of the Central Office. The Transparent Animal Rooms drop has been completed, and the launch of the Space Time Rooms is anticipated soon, marking the next phase in the evolution of Black City.

Launched by MOCA (Museum of Crypto Art), ROOMs are engine agnostic, virtual-world-interoperable, 1-of-1 architectural objects. They are decentralized extensions of the Museum itself. With a ROOM NFT, holders can independently and permissionless-ly curate, exhibit, and deploy art exhibitions, shows, and spatial experiences.

During the launch of Transparent Animal Rooms, we employed an array of innovative and disruptive marketing videos to create widespread awareness and excitement about both the unique designs and the software product itself. These videos were strategically shared across various social media platforms, leveraging their wide reach and engagement potential to maximize the impact.

As the second Room series by decentralize, Transparent Animal Rooms are primarily motivated by the thought of pulling themselves back to a point in the volume without losing their silhouettes since museum buildings are a typology that nearly transforms into an artwork itself with an attitude that stands out very much as a typology. However, this retreat is "humble" enough to allow users to explore artworks from many angles while also realizing that the museums themselves are a creation of art or design.

In terms of both form and function, it is a fusion of two concepts in this quadrilateral series as well as an investigation into how these concepts might be infused with equal or varying energies in each design. The first strategy investigates the informal relationships of the arcs inspired by Untitled xyz's works. The second one is based on a sketch of Le Corbusier's Museum of Unlimited Growth, which resembles a labyrinth that spins over the core to create functional corridors.

The 64-slot Museum of Unlimited Growth is a transparent digital recreation of Corbusier's drawing. The 32-slot Spyder Mosk and the 16-slot Spyder Mosk Piccolo explore the idea of an arch-based structure, similar to Salvador Dalí's elephants in mosque proportions, with the larger one also incorporating a labyrinthine spiral system. Differently representing the blending of these two concepts is the 8-Slot Fly Club.
